children.name: Meles
children.description: The true badgers of the Palearctic — powerfully built, short-legged diggers with the iconic black-and-white striped face. Eurasian Badgers are the largest land predators native to Britain and among the most social of all mustelids, living in multi-family clans in elaborate underground tunnel systems called setts that may be used continuously for centuries. Their foraging leaves characteristic conical snout-holes across pasture, and an earthy, musky sett on a woodland edge is one of the most evocative smells of the European night.

children.name: Arctonyx
children.description: Hog badgers — large, pig-snouted badgers of South and East Asia with a distinctive white throat and elongated, flexible snout used to root through soil and leaf litter for invertebrates, tubers, and small vertebrates. They are the largest members of the badger lineage and are largely solitary and nocturnal, inhabiting forests and grasslands from northeast India to China.

children.name: Mellivora
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Honey Badger — pound for pound one of the most ferocious animals on Earth. The Honey Badger has loose, thick skin that allows it to twist around and bite an attacker holding it from behind, and it is resistant to bee stings, porcupine quills, and many snake venoms. It has been reliably observed raiding beehives, eating cobras, and tearing open concrete enclosures in zoos. Its fearlessness is not bravado; it has almost no natural predators.

children.name: Gulo
children.description: The wolverine — the largest terrestrial mustelid, built like a small bear with the temperament of something considerably larger. Wolverines range enormous territories across the boreal forests and Arctic tundra of Scandinavia, Russia, and North America, scavenging frozen carcasses with jaws strong enough to crush bone and even frozen meat. In Scandinavia they are a totemic animal of the wilderness, feared and respected by reindeer herders whose semi-domestic herds they occasionally prey upon.

children.name: Martes
children.description: The martens — slender, tree-climbing mustelids with semi-retractable claws and a bounding gait through forest canopy. The European Pine Marten is a forest specialist that has recovered strongly in parts of Scandinavia and Britain, partly because it outcompetes the introduced Grey Squirrel. The Sable was so relentlessly hunted for its extraordinarily soft, dark fur that it drove Russian eastward expansion across Siberia; Cossack expeditions were essentially sable-hunting enterprises.

children.name: Martes americana
children.description: A lithe, agile tree-climber of North American boreal forests, closely related to the European Pine Marten and occupying a similar ecological niche. It is an important furbearer whose populations declined sharply with trapping and old-growth logging but have partially recovered with forest succession; IUCN status: Least Concern.

children.name: Mustela
children.description: Weasels, stoats, and polecats — the smallest mustelids and among the smallest carnivores. They are specialist hunters of small mammals, able to pursue mice and voles into their own burrows, and can take prey several times their own body weight. The stoat turns white in winter (becoming the 'ermine' of royal robes) — one of the few Palearctic mammals with a seasonal colour change — while the Least Weasel is the smallest carnivorous mammal on Earth.

children.name: Mustela frenata
children.description: The most widely distributed weasel in the Americas, ranging from southern Canada to Bolivia. It is a highly adaptable predator of small mammals and birds, and like the stoat it turns white in winter in northern populations; IUCN status: Least Concern.

children.name: Mustela nigripes
children.description: North America's only native ferret, once considered extinct and now the subject of one of the most intensive recovery programmes on the continent, reintroduced to the Great Plains from a captive population of just 18 individuals. Virtually dependent on prairie dogs for food and burrows; IUCN status: Endangered.

children.name: Mustela altaica
children.description: A high-altitude weasel of the steppes and rocky slopes from the Altai Mountains to the Himalayas and western China, preying chiefly on pikas, voles, and small birds. It is well adapted to cold, thin-air environments and is largely crepuscular; IUCN status: Near Threatened.

children.name: Vormela
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Marbled Polecat — one of the most strikingly patterned of all mustelids, with a mottled yellow-and-brown back and a black mask and belly. Found in arid grasslands and semi-deserts from southeastern Europe to China, it is a specialist predator of ground squirrels and voles. When threatened it arches its back, curls its tail, and displays the bold contrasting colours as a warning — possibly mimicking the warning display of skunks with which it shares common ancestry.

children.name: Lutra
children.description: The Eurasian Otter — a supremely adapted semi-aquatic hunter, with dense waterproof fur, webbed feet, closeable nostrils and ears, and eyes adapted to see underwater. Otters were almost extirpated from much of Europe by hunting and pesticide pollution in the 20th century but have made a spectacular comeback following the ban of organochlorine pesticides and legal protection. The sight of an otter sliding into a river at dusk is once again possible across Scandinavia and much of the British Isles.

children.name: Aonyx
children.description: Clawless otters — Asian and African otters with reduced or absent claws and highly dexterous bare-skinned fingers, used to feel for crabs, molluscs, and frogs under rocks and in mud. The Asian Small-clawed Otter is the smallest otter species and one of the most social, living in extended family groups with complex vocal communication and cooperative foraging.

children.name: Lontra
children.description: New World river otters — four species distributed from Canada to Patagonia, filling the semi-aquatic freshwater predator niche vacated by Lutra in the Americas. They share the same streamlined body plan and dense waterproof fur as their Old World cousins but evolved independently in the Neotropics. The North American River Otter has recovered across much of its former range following reintroduction programmes and water-quality improvements.

children.name: Lontra canadensis
children.description: A highly mobile semi-aquatic predator of rivers, lakes, and coastal waters across North America, feeding on fish, crayfish, and amphibians. Extirpated from large parts of its range by trapping and pollution, it has been reintroduced successfully in many US states; IUCN status: Least Concern.

children.name: Lontra longicaudis
children.description: Widespread across freshwater habitats from Mexico to Uruguay, this otter is sensitive to water quality and human disturbance and has disappeared from many river systems due to hunting and habitat degradation. It is predominantly crepuscular and nocturnal; IUCN status: Near Threatened.

children.name: Pteronura
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Giant Otter — the world's longest mustelid, reaching up to 1.8 m from nose to tail-tip. Giant Otters are highly social, living in noisy, cooperative family groups of up to eight individuals that jointly defend territories along South American river systems. They were hunted almost to extinction for their plush, velvety pelts in the 20th century and remain among South America's most endangered large mammals.

children.name: Pteronura brasiliensis
children.description: The world's largest otter, inhabiting slow-moving rivers, lakes, and swamps of the Amazon and Orinoco basins. It is a diurnal, apex aquatic predator capable of catching caimans and anacondas. Populations were reduced by over 80% by the fur trade before protection; IUCN status: Endangered.

children.name: Enhydra
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Sea Otter — the only fully marine mustelid and the heaviest member of the family. Sea Otters are renowned for using rocks as anvils to crack open shellfish, making them one of the few non-primate tool-using mammals. They spend almost their entire lives at sea, rarely coming ashore, and maintain body temperature without blubber by trapping air bubbles in the densest fur of any mammal — up to a million hairs per square centimetre.

children.name: Enhydra lutris
children.description: A keystone species of North Pacific kelp forest ecosystems, controlling sea urchin populations that would otherwise overgraze kelp. Hunted to near-extinction for its extraordinarily dense fur during the maritime fur trade; surviving populations in Alaska and California are recovering. IUCN status: Endangered.

children.name: Taxidea
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the American Badger — the New World counterpart to the Eurasian Badger, though more closely related to martens than to Meles. It is a powerful, low-slung digger of open grasslands and shrublands, specialising in excavating ground squirrels, prairie dogs, and pocket gophers from their burrows with astonishing speed. Coyotes and American Badgers are well documented as cooperative hunters, one flushing prey above ground while the other waits at burrow entrances.

children.name: Taxidea taxus
children.description: A robust, flat-bodied burrowing predator of the Great Plains, Great Basin, and western grasslands of North America, with powerful forelimbs and long claws adapted for rapid digging. It is a solitary, largely nocturnal hunter of fossorial rodents and plays an important ecosystem role as a prolific excavator; IUCN status: Least Concern.

children.name: Pekania
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Fisher — a large, dark-furred marten of North American boreal and mixed forests. Despite its name the Fisher rarely eats fish; it is one of the few specialist predators of porcupines, attacking their unquilled face in a prolonged circling assault. Fishers were trapped to extinction across much of New England and the Midwest for their luxurious fur but have been reintroduced and are now recovering.

children.name: Pekania pennanti
children.description: A large, solitary mustelid of mature coniferous and mixed forests from the Sierra Nevada to Nova Scotia, notable as one of the very few predators capable of killing a porcupine. It was nearly extirpated by the fur trade and has been successfully reintroduced to many areas; IUCN status: Least Concern.
